denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

This notice concerns the prior authorization request submitted by your neurologist for continued coverage of your ongoing maintenance infusion therapy. Your provider's request was received at our utilization management center on June 1, 2026 at 2:14 PM, and this determination was issued the same day at 2:17 PM.

Cigna is unable to approve the request. Continuation of this therapy requires an active prior authorization on file. The prior authorization that previously covered your treatment has lapsed, and our records do not reflect a current, approved authorization for the continued service. A new prior authorization request, accompanied by current clinical documentation, must be submitted and approved before any further doses will be covered under your plan.

This determination is based on the absence of an active authorization on file and does not by itself state a conclusion about the clinical appropriateness of your treatment.

If you disagree with this determination, you may request an internal appeal by calling the number on your member identification card. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization once internal appeal rights are exhausted. Your provider may submit a new prior authorization request at any time.

This letter addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 58-year-old man with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, diagnosed in his mid-40s and clinically and radiologically stable for several years on maintenance ocrelizumab infusions every six months, with no relapses, no new or enhancing lesions on his most recent MRI, and no features of secondary progression. His neurologist submitted current records — the treatment history, the stable MRI report, and the EDSS examination — together with the re-authorization request for the next scheduled infusion. The prior authorization lapsed for administrative reasons at the plan's renewal cycle, not because of any change in his condition or any gap in treatment need; interrupting ocrelizumab risks disease reactivation and a rebound of disability. The denial was generated three minutes after the request was received, at 2:17 PM the same afternoon, a turnaround too short for any reviewer to have read the submitted documentation. The notice does not describe the member's ERISA appeal rights, including the right to bring a civil action.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field; G35 RRMS is neurology; ocrelizumab continuation realistic; rationale instantiates continuation_of_care_lapsed; age 58 in band.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Self-funded PA denial of a continuation ocrelizumab infusion citing a lapsed authorization at renewal is a common real-world defect; 3-minute turnaround and ERISA-disclosure omission are authentic exploitable flaws.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Branded Cigna with procedural cadence and no wrong-insurer tells, but administrative lapse denial cites no Cigna-specific MCG/Medical Necessity Guidelines.
insurer_voice.improvement: Reference Cigna MCG / Medical Necessity Guidelines.

denial_logic.reasoning: Authentically shoddy continuation_of_care_lapsed: denies purely on absent active authorization, disclaims clinical judgment, no lapse window or threshold; rubber-stamp 3-minute turnaround; appealable, not hallucinated.

clinical_realism.reasoning: Ocrelizumab q6-month maintenance, stability by relapse/MRI/EDSS, rebound risk on interruption; correct indication, no biology violations.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Ocrelizumab is FDA-approved standard DMT for relapsing MS; clean match.

diversity_delta.reasoning: Shares G35 with one neighbour but distinct drug class (IV anti-CD20 vs oral S1P ozanimod), insurer, and continuation-lapse trajectory; no near-duplicate.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.076 (nearest=case_116_cigna_mednec), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: No banned topics; adult 58M maintenance DMT; self-funded commercial ERISA plan, no government-payer framing.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Gender, diagnosis, drug, timestamps stable across documents; 'documentation must be submitted' vs records already submitted is the appealable falsehood, not an unstable fact; internally consistent.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Formal insurer boilerplate and dense chart-style prose; no GPT vocabulary, listicles, or meta-commentary.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural Cigna register, administrative basis, no apology/marketing; clinical context plain factual, risk stated neutrally.

financial_auditor.reasoning: Silent on cost liability (standard practice); no dollar figures; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation.

legal_auditor.reasoning: Generic appeal/external-review rights, no hallucinated statutes, no Medicare framing; ERISA civil-action disclosure strategically omitted as realistic attackable imperfection.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 58M with RRMS diagnosed mid-40s and stable is epidemiologically plausible (late/older male MS documented); consistent male pronouns and 'Mr.' salutation.

scope_guard.reasoning: Cigna in-scope, self-funded commercial ERISA, adult 58, member-facing appeal rights only; ERISA civil-action is descriptive, not a live-filing claim.

date_sanity.reasoning: June 1 2026 2:14 PM received / 2:17 PM issued are consistent same-day; mid-40s diagnosis and several-years stability stack plausibly; no contradictions.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Administrative denial with only generic internal-record references, no hallucinated alphanumeric codes; realistically vague.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Rubber-stamp 3-minute turnaround proves no review; complete current records show stable medically-necessary maintenance; ERISA disclosure omitted; administrative-only lapse. Easy multi-vector appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Three-minute turnaround (2:14 PM to 2:17 PM) proves no meaningful review of the submitted clinical documentation occurred
Notice omits ERISA appeal rights, including the right to bring a civil action under 29 U.S.C. 1133 / 502(a)
Denial is administrative-only despite complete current records showing stable, medically-necessary maintenance whose interruption risks disease reactivation
strong_defenses: An active prior authorization is a legitimate, plan-defined coverage prerequisite, and the insurer correctly notes the prior PA had lapsed and invites resubmission
